intro: Ruby (Hebrew odem or peninim, the precise identification uncertain) was among the precious stones valued in the ancient Near East. The first stone in the high priest's breastplate (Exodus 28:17) was an odem, traditionally translated ruby or carnelian, set in the first row. Lamentations 4:7 compares the Nazarites, whose bodies had been more ruddy than rubies (peninim), in their former purity, with their darkened state in the desolation of the exile. Proverbs 3:15 and 8:11 use rubies as the measure against which wisdom is incomparably greater: "She is more precious than rubies: and all the things thou canst desire are not to be compared unto her." The precise identification of the Hebrew gemstone terms with modern stones remains debated by scholars.
